items.question_text: Who is covered by this order?

choices.text: All ATO personnel and anyone using ATO directives.
Only certified controllers currently working traffic.
Only FAA employees, not outside users of ATO directives.
Only facility managers and headquarters staff.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-2, this order applies to all ATO personnel and anyone using ATO directives.

items.situation: A user says the order cannot apply because they are not ATO personnel, even though they are relying on ATO directives to do the work.
items.para_context: The order applies to all ATO personnel and anyone using ATO directives.

choices.text: Treat the order as applicable because it also applies to anyone using ATO directives.
Treat the order as inapplicable unless the person is currently assigned to an ATC operating position.
Apply the order only if the user is a supervisor or manager.
Ignore the directive entirely because only ATO personnel may use it.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-2, applicability is not limited to ATO personnel; it also covers anyone using ATO directives.

items.question_text: Where is this order published for users to find it online?

choices.text: On the FAA Air Traffic Plans and Publications website and the FAA Orders & Notices website.
Only on each local facility intranet page.
Only in the pilot training portal.
Only through the AJV-P correspondence mailbox.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-3, the order is available on the FAA Air Traffic Plans and Publications website and the FAA Orders & Notices website.

items.question_text: What did this edition cancel when it was issued?

choices.text: FAA Order JO 7110.65AA, dated April 20, 2023, and all changes to it.
Only FAA Order JO 7110.65BB Change 1.
All letters of agreement currently in effect.
Only local facility supplements to the order.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-4, FAA Order JO 7110.65AA, dated April 20, 2023, and all changes to it are canceled.

items.question_text: Where are the significant changes to the order identified?

choices.text: In the Explanation of Changes page or pages.
Only in each chapter heading.
Only in the distribution paragraph.
Only in table footnotes.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-5, the significant changes are identified in the Explanation of Changes page or pages.

items.situation: A coworker wants to throw away the Explanation of Changes pages right after skimming them, even though the basic order will remain in use for a long period.
items.para_context: It is advisable to retain the Explanation of Changes page or pages throughout the duration of the basic order.

choices.text: Retain the Explanation of Changes page or pages throughout the duration of the basic order.
Discard them immediately because the order text always repeats every significant change in place.
Keep them only until the next shift change.
Replace them with local notes because retaining the pages is discouraged.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-5, it is advisable to retain the Explanation of Changes pages for the duration of the basic order.

items.question_text: What does the cutoff date for completion represent in the publication schedule?

choices.text: The deadline for a proposed change to be fully coordinated and signed.
The first day a facility may start drafting a proposed change.
The date local software must be installed at every facility.
The deadline for pilots to comment on a proposed change.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-6, the cutoff date for completion is the deadline for a proposed change to be fully coordinated and signed.

items.situation: You are proposing a change to the order, and someone suggests waiting until just before the cutoff date to start the submission because the review should move quickly.
items.para_context: Change initiators must submit proposed changes well in advance of the cutoff date because review and coordination often take several months after initial submission.

choices.text: Submit the proposed change well in advance of the cutoff date because review and coordination often take several months.
Wait until the cutoff date itself because only the final signature date matters.
Ignore the cutoff date as long as the proposal is drafted before the effective date.
Submit the change after publication, because AIRAC timing applies only to charts and not to this order.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-6, publication is tied to AIRAC dates and proposed changes must be submitted well ahead of the cutoff date because coordination takes time.

items.question_text: When should this order be available on the FAA website before its effective date?

choices.text: 30 days before the effective date.
On the effective date.
7 days before the effective date.
Only after each facility confirms receipt.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-7, the order is available on the FAA website 30 days before its effective date.

items.situation: Your FAA facility is less than 30 days from the effective date and still has not received the order change package.
items.para_context: An FAA facility that has not received the order or changes at least 30 days before the effective date must notify its service area office distribution officer.

choices.text: Notify the service area office distribution officer.
Wait until the effective date passes before reporting it.
Send the request only to the AJV-P correspondence mailbox.
Ask pilots whether they already have the new text and use that instead.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-7, an FAA facility that has not received the order or changes at least 30 days before the effective date notifies its service area office distribution officer.

items.situation: A controller wants to recommend a procedural change and asks where the proposal should go first.
items.para_context: Recommended procedural changes are submitted to facility management before moving through the formal process.

choices.text: Submit the recommended change to facility management first.
Send it directly to pilots for informal adoption before management sees it.
Post it publicly first, then send it through FAA channels if it gains support.
Skip facility management and send it only to the nearest district office.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-8, personnel should submit recommended changes in procedures to facility management.

items.question_text: What should an electronic proposed-change submission include?

choices.text: A description of the recommended change and the proposed language to be used in the order.
Only the paragraph number and a note that a change is desired.
Only a marked-up page with no written explanation.
Only a statement that local software will eventually be adapted.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-8, electronic submissions should include both a description of the recommended change and the proposed language to be used in the order.

items.instruction: Put the request-routing steps in order.

steps.text: The Air Traffic Manager submits the request in writing to the District General Manager.
The Service Area Director of Air Traffic Operations reviews the request to determine whether an interpretation or clarification is needed.
The District General Manager forwards the request in writing through the Operations Support Group to the Service Area Director of Air Traffic Operations.
If needed, the Service Area Director of Air Traffic Operations forwards the request to AJV-P.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-9, the AJT field-personnel request chain runs from the Air Traffic Manager to the District General Manager, through the OSG to the Service Area Director, and then to AJV-P if an interpretation or clarification is needed.

items.situation: A DoD requestor wants a clarification of this order and plans to email AJV-P directly without using the military chain of command.
items.para_context: DoD requests are routed in writing through the chain of command to the respective Military Service Headquarters, which then forwards valid requests to AJV-P.

choices.text: Route the request in writing through the appropriate chain of command to the respective Military Service Headquarters, which then submits valid requests to AJV-P.
Let the requestor send it directly to AJV-P because all DoD requests bypass the chain of command.
Tell the requestor to send it only to the local service area office.
Wait for a pilot complaint before starting the routing process.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-9, DoD requests are submitted in writing through the appropriate chain of command to the respective Military Service Headquarters, which then forwards valid requests to AJV-P.

items.question_text: How may a letter of agreement affect the minima in this order?

choices.text: It only supplements the order and may not set lower minima unless the stated military-authority exception applies.
It replaces the order whenever two facilities sign it.
It may set any lower minima as long as both facilities concur verbally.
It may never address procedures or minima at all.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-10, LOAs only supplement the order, and their minima may not be less than the order's minima except for the stated military-aircraft authorization case.

items.situation: Two facilities have been using a jointly applied arrival procedure based on a verbal understanding, and one supervisor wants to add reduced separation language locally without documenting it.
items.para_context: Jointly applied procedures or minima requiring cooperation between facilities must be documented in an LOA, and the LOA may not reduce minima below the order except for the stated military exception.

choices.text: Document the jointly applied procedure in an LOA and do not use minima below the order unless the stated military-authority exception applies.
Keep the verbal procedure in place because both facilities already know how it works.
Treat the local memo as a replacement for the order once both supervisors agree.
Use the reduced minima now and write the LOA only if someone later asks for it.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-10, jointly applied procedures or minima requiring cooperation between facilities must be documented in an LOA, and the LOA cannot reduce minima below the order except under the stated military authority exception.

items.question_text: What is required before a waiver request when military operations or facilities are involved?

choices.text: Prior approval by the appropriate military headquarters.
Prior approval by the nearest FAA regional counsel office.
Only local facility concurrence.
A completed LOA, but no military approval.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-11, if military operations or facilities are involved, prior approval by the appropriate military headquarters is required for the subsequent waiver request.

items.situation: An unusual local requirement will need a waiver, and the proposal involves military operations. A supervisor wants to send the waiver package forward before coordinating with the appropriate military headquarters.
items.para_context: Military involvement requires prior approval by the appropriate military headquarters before the waiver request goes forward.

choices.text: Obtain prior approval from the appropriate military headquarters before the subsequent waiver request proceeds.
Submit the waiver first and ask the military headquarters to concur only after the waiver is approved.
Skip military coordination because FAA approval alone is sufficient whenever the procedure is temporary.
Document the plan in a facility note and treat that as an approved waiver.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-11, military involvement requires prior approval by the appropriate headquarters before the waiver request goes forward.

items.question_text: Who owns and executes the ATO Safety Management System?

choices.text: All employees at all levels of the ATO.
Only the safety office and designated SMS practitioners.
Only service center quality-control personnel.
Only facility managers and district staff.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-12, the ATO SMS gives responsibility for owning and executing the SMS to all employees at all levels of the ATO.

items.situation: You identify a recurring safety hazard, and a coworker says SMS belongs only to the safety office so line personnel should just keep working around it.
items.para_context: All ATO employees share responsibility for owning and executing SMS and for continuously improving safety.

choices.text: Treat SMS as a responsibility shared by all ATO employees and use the available SMS processes or resources to address the hazard.
Ignore the hazard unless a formal SMS practitioner is physically present at the position.
Assume the hazard is outside SMS because only equipment issues belong in the system.
Wait until the next order change is published before reporting or addressing the hazard.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-12, all ATO employees share responsibility for owning and executing the SMS and for continuously improving safety.

items.question_text: When a reference names an FAA regional office organization outside the Air Traffic Organization, which region should the facility contact?

choices.text: The FAA region where the facility is physically located.
The region where the service area office is located.
Whichever region most recently issued a directive on the subject.
No regional contact is needed because those references are only informational.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-13, the facility should contact the FAA region where the facility is physically located.

items.situation: A paragraph references a non-ATO FAA office, and someone wants to call the region tied to the service area office instead of the region where the facility actually sits.
items.para_context: The facility contacts the FAA region where it is physically located when the reference is to a non-ATO regional office organization.

choices.text: Contact the FAA region where the facility is physically located.
Always contact the service-area region, because physical location does not matter.
Contact whichever region handled the last pilot complaint.
Wait for headquarters to assign a region before making any contact.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-13, references to FAA non-air-traffic organizations are handled through the region where the facility is physically located, not the service-area region.

items.question_text: What are organizations responsible for when this order changes?

choices.text: Viewing, downloading, and subscribing to receive email notifications through the FAA website.
Waiting for headquarters to mail a printed replacement to every individual user.
Relying on pilots to report that a change has been published.
Checking only local supplements, because electronic distribution is optional.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-14, organizations are responsible for viewing, downloading, and subscribing to receive email notifications when changes occur to the order.

items.situation: A facility assumes that once the order exists online, there is no need to subscribe for notifications or actively check for changes.
items.para_context: Organizations are responsible for viewing, downloading, and subscribing to receive email notifications when changes occur to the order.

choices.text: Actively view and download the order as needed and subscribe for email notifications when changes occur.
Wait for a paper mailing because organizations are not responsible for tracking electronic updates.
Rely on verbal notification from adjacent facilities instead of subscribing through the FAA website.
Ignore subscriptions because only headquarters offices are expected to track revisions.

items.explanation: Per 1-1-14, organizations themselves are responsible for viewing, downloading, and subscribing for change notifications.

items.instruction: Match each term with its meaning in this order.

pairs.definition: futurity, not a requirement for the application of a procedure

items.question_text: In this order, what does the word "will" mean?

choices.text: Futurity, not a requirement for applying a procedure.
A mandatory action that must be completed exactly as written.
A prohibited action.
An optional action that controllers may ignore entirely.

items.explanation: Per 1-2-1, "will" means futurity, not a requirement for the application of a procedure.

items.situation: A military pilot says the aircraft is going tactical and will leave ATC frequency while insisting that this alone automatically establishes due regard.
items.para_context: The phrase going tactical does not by itself establish due regard, and the required understanding and conditions must actually exist.

choices.text: Do not treat "going tactical" as due regard by itself; ensure the required understanding and due-regard conditions are actually established before the aircraft leaves frequency.
Assume due regard automatically applies whenever a pilot says "going tactical."
Convert the flight to VFR immediately because due regard exists only in visual conditions.
Continue normal IFR separation after the aircraft leaves frequency because due regard transfers separation responsibility back to ATC.

items.explanation: Per 1-2-1, due regard has specific conditions, and a pilot's use of the phrase "going tactical" does not by itself establish due regard.

items.question_text: What does the note say about protected airspace that does not overlap?

choices.text: Applicable lateral separation is ensured.
The aircraft are automatically on reciprocal courses.
Vertical separation is no longer required.
The course classification no longer matters.

items.explanation: Per the note in 1-2-2, if the protected airspace of two aircraft does not overlap, applicable lateral separation is ensured.

items.situation: Two aircraft will cross with an angular difference of about 100 degrees, and their protected airspaces intersect. Another controller wants to classify them as reciprocal courses.
items.para_context: Crossing courses have an angular difference of 45 through 135 degrees inclusive when the protected airspaces intersect as defined.

choices.text: Treat them as crossing courses because the angular difference is within 45 through 135 degrees inclusive.
Treat them as reciprocal courses because any intersecting protected airspaces make the courses opposite.
Treat them as same courses because any angle under 180 degrees is the same course.
Ignore the course category because only altitude matters once protected airspaces intersect.

items.explanation: Per 1-2-2, intersecting courses with an angular difference of 45 through 135 degrees inclusive are crossing courses.

items.question_text: What is the purpose of a note in this order?

choices.text: To present statements of fact or prefatory or explanatory material related to directive text.
To create a mandatory requirement that overrides directive text.
To identify only military-only procedures.
To replace references to other publications.

items.explanation: Per 1-2-3, notes set forth statements of fact or material of a prefatory or explanatory nature relating to directive material.

items.question_text: The term "note" refers to statements of fact or prefatory or explanatory material relating to directive material.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Per 1-2-3, notes are statements of fact or prefatory or explanatory material relating to directive material.

items.question_text: What does a reference do in this order?

choices.text: It directs attention to an additional or supporting source of information.
It creates a new mandatory procedure not found anywhere else.
It identifies phraseology that must always be spoken verbatim.
It shows which chapters are canceled by the order.

items.explanation: Per 1-2-4, references direct attention to an additional or supporting source of information.

items.question_text: In this order, references direct attention to an additional or supporting source of information.

choices.text: True
False

items.explanation: Per 1-2-4, references direct attention to an additional or supporting source of information.

items.instruction: Match each annotation to what it tells you.

pairs.definition: prescribed words or phrases to be used in communications
sample of the way associated phraseology will be used
the remaining information in the paragraph requires wake turbulence procedures
the paragraph applies only to the designated type of facility

items.question_text: How should an example that is not preceded by specific prescribed phraseology be treated?

choices.text: Its exact text is not mandatory, but the wording should approximate the example to the extent practical.
Its exact text is mandatory and must always be used verbatim.
It may be ignored because examples have no training value.
It applies only when a paragraph is marked TERMINAL.

items.explanation: Per 1-2-5, an example not preceded by specific prescribed phraseology is not mandatory verbatim, but the wording is expected to approximate the example to the extent practical.

items.situation: You use the prescribed phraseology for a procedure, but the pilot still sounds confused and may not have understood the content.
items.para_context: After using prescribed phraseology first, controllers may rephrase the message as needed to ensure the content is understood and should use good judgment with nonstandard wording.

choices.text: After using the prescribed phraseology first, rephrase the message as needed to ensure the content is understood, using good judgment with any nonstandard wording.
Repeat the same words indefinitely because any rephrasing is prohibited once phraseology is prescribed.
Drop the prescribed phraseology entirely and use only casual wording from the start.
Stop issuing the instruction because examples are not expected to resemble actual controller wording.

items.explanation: Per 1-2-5, controllers may rephrase after first using prescribed phraseology if needed to ensure the content is understood, but should use good judgment with nonstandard wording.

items.instruction: Use Table 1-2-1 to answer this question.
items.question_text: Which abbreviation means Approval Request?

choices.text: APREQ
APR
ATIS
ATC

items.explanation: Table 1-2-1 shows that APREQ means Approval Request.

items.question_text: What does ATIS stand for in Table 1-2-1?

choices.text: Automatic Terminal Information Service.
Air Traffic Information Standardization.
Automatic Traffic Integration System.
Aeronautical Terminal Identification Service.

items.explanation: Table 1-2-1 shows that ATIS means Automatic Terminal Information Service.
